coredata.dc:title: On-demand release of CO in dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogels for wound dressing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jing
Shi, Fengjun
Lu, Qiqi
Ye, Wei
Liu, Sen
Liu, Jingjing
Zhang, Chao
Zhao, Jie
Ming, Weihua
coredata.dc:description: Gaseous signaling molecules, especially carbon monoxide (CO), hold promising potential for disease management. The therapeutic efficacy of CO is closely tied to its concentration, however, maintaining it at optimal levels for both efficacy and safety is highly challenging. To address this, we designed a dual-responsive (pH/red light) nanocomposite hydrogel for on-demand CO release. We first synthesized a hybrid nanocomposite (CaCO3@AgCCN) comprising a CO2 donor (CaCO3) and a photocatalyst (Ag3PO4-decorated carbon dot g-C3N4, AgCCN) capable of converting CO2 to CO. The size of CaCO3 particles was approximately 40 nm, while that of AgCCN was around 150 nm in this nanocomposite. CaCO3@AgCCN was then incorporated into chitosan (CS) to form a nanocomposite hydrogel. This nanocomposite hydrogel could respond to a mildly acidic environment due to bacterial growth, generating CO2 exactly where it is needed (the wound site), which would be subsequently catalytically converted to CO by AgCCN under 630-nm red light illumination to facilitate wound healing. The generated CO, readily controlled by adjusting the CaCO3@AgCCN content in the nanocomposite hydrogel and the red-light illumination time (the CO concentration reaching 4.7 μM after 10-min illumination), has demonstrated strong bactericidal and anti-inflammatory effects, both essential in facilitating wound healing as shown in both in vitro and in vivo studies. Coupled with satisfactory biocompatibility, this dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel appears to hold great promise for safe and effective applications of CO in biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Carbon monoxide
Antimicrobial
Wound healing
Biocompatibility

Preparation and characterization of nanocomposite hydrogels
Detection of CO2 and CO release
Water swelling and stress-strain property of nanocomposite hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of pH/red light dual-responsive CaCO3@AgCCN/CS hydrogel and its application in helping treat wound infection.

(a,b) TEM images of AgCCN and CaCO3@AgCCN. (c–f) Elemental mappings of CaCO3@AgCCN. (g) XRD patterns of CCN, AgCCN, CaCO3@AgCCN, and the standard card No. 47-1743 (CaCO3) and No. 06-0505 (Ag3PO4). (h) FTIR spectra of CCN and AgCCN. (i) EDX spectrum of CaCO3@AgCCN.

(a) SEM image and (b) EDX elemental mapping of CA/CS-20 %. Hemoglobin assay for in vitro CO detection: absorption spectra of CA/CS-20 % and reduced hemoglobin (c) with 630-nm light illumination and (d) without 630-nm light illumination, (e) released CO amount as a function of time for different samples.

(a) Photographs of S. aureus and E. coli colonies grown on broth agar plates in the presence of various hydrogels. (b) SEM images of adhesive S. aureus and E. coli on the surface of CA/CS hydrogels without or with 630 nm light illumination.

(a) Change of fluorescence spectra of COP-1 in bacterial suspension (the inset is the corresponding luminescent photographs at 0 min and 10 min respectively). (b) The fluorescence intensity changes with the illumination time in two different media. (c) Schematic of on-demand release of CO from red light/pH dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogels and its antibacterial mechanism.

(a) Live/dead staining images of L929 fibroblasts after being treated with various hydrogels (CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, and CA/CS-20 % with 630-nm red light illumination), and (b) the corresponding cell viability. (c) The corresponding hemolysis ratios of various hydrogels.

In vivo evaluation of various groups (CA/CS-20 % without illumination, and CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, CA/CS-20 % with 630 nm illumination) on an infectious rat full-thickness defect model. (a) Representative photographs after different treatment times. Scale bar is 4 mm. (b) The colony count from the mouse wounds on the seventh day. (c) Diagram of the wound areas after treatments on days 0, 1, 3, 5, and 7. (d) The corresponding relative bacterial survival rate on the seventh day. (e) Weight change of the infected mouse over time.
H&E staining of the wound tissue after 7 days of various treatments, the typical arrows indicate inflammatory cells (red arrows) and newly formed blood vessels (green arrows).

head.ce:title: On-demand release of CO in dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogels for wound dressing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Georgia Southern University, PO Box 8064, Statesboro, GA 30460, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Georgia Southern University, PO Box 8064, Statesboro, GA 30460, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gaseous signaling molecules, especially carbon monoxide (CO), hold promising potential for disease management. The therapeutic efficacy of CO is closely tied to its concentration, however, maintaining it at optimal levels for both efficacy and safety is highly challenging. To address this, we designed a dual-responsive (pH/red light) nanocomposite hydrogel for on-demand CO release. We first synthesized a hybrid nanocomposite (CaCO3@AgCCN) comprising a CO2 donor (CaCO3) and a photocatalyst (Ag3PO4-decorated carbon dot g-C3N4, AgCCN) capable of converting CO2 to CO. The size of CaCO3 particles was approximately 40 nm, while that of AgCCN was around 150 nm in this nanocomposite. CaCO3@AgCCN was then incorporated into chitosan (CS) to form a nanocomposite hydrogel. This nanocomposite hydrogel could respond to a mildly acidic environment due to bacterial growth, generating CO2 exactly where it is needed (the wound site), which would be subsequently catalytically converted to CO by AgCCN under 630-nm red light illumination to facilitate wound healing. The generated CO, readily controlled by adjusting the CaCO3@AgCCN content in the nanocomposite hydrogel and the red-light illumination time (the CO concentration reaching 4.7 μM after 10-min illumination), has demonstrated strong bactericidal and anti-inflammatory effects, both essential in facilitating wound healing as shown in both in vitro and in vivo studies. Coupled with satisfactory biocompatibility, this dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel appears to hold great promise for safe and effective applications of CO in biomedical fields.

Multicomponent nanomaterials (CaCO3@AgCCN) consisting of CaCO3 and Z-scheme photocatalyst (AgCCN) were constructed, which were further loaded into chitosan (CS) matrix to establish a pH/red light dual-responsive composite hydrogel (CaCO3@AgCCN/CS). The loading of CaCO3 endowed the hydrogel with the capacity to respond to the mildly acidic environment of bacterial growth, enhancing CO2 content exactly at the wound site. Simultaneously, AgCCN can catalyze the conversion of CO2 to CO with a 630 nm illumination, precisely aiding wound healing.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traumatic infection has emerged as one of the most pressing medical challenges, leading to significant rates of disability and mortality worldwide. Traditional wound dressings have played an important role in promoting wound healing; however, their removal often results in skin tissue adhesion, causing secondary wounds and increased patient discomfort [1,2]. Hydrogel-based biomaterials, with interconnected macroporous networks, flexibility, elasticity, and hydrating capabilities, have garnered attention for potential applications [3]. Notably, these hydrogel materials can maintain a moist environment and prevent tissue adhesion during wound treatment [4,5]. Meanwhile, combining antibacterial agents, such as antibiotics, zinc oxide, and quaternary ammonium salts with hydrogels, has proven effective in resisting pathogen invasion and avoiding inflammation [6-8]. Nevertheless, the inevitable bacterial resistance and systemic toxicity triggered by these frequently used antimicrobials may impede the wound healing process. Moreover, certain antibacterial agents with uncontrolled release behavior would compromise the anti-inflammatory efficacy, increase biotoxicity, and delay wound healing [9-11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop smart hydrogel dressings that offer minimal toxicity and on-demand antibacterial properties.

Recently, stimuli-responsive antimicrobial materials have gained attention due to their potential for controlled and effective antibacterial action under specific conditions such as light, temperature, pH, and enzymes [12-14]. Among them, the light-controlled release of gaseous signaling molecules (H2S, CO, and NO) for wound healing has been widely studied, owing to its superior permeability and controllability [15-17]. Specifically, CO possesses various biological functions, including anti-inflammatory, anti-apoptosis, anti-atherosclerosis, and cell protection, and it can also kill drug-resistant bacteria without causing drug resistance [18,19]. However, the therapeutic efficacy of CO is strictly dependent on its concentration [20]. Research has shown that the therapeutic concentration of carbon monoxide is 250–500 ppm [21,22]. A low concentration of CO has very limited therapeutic effect, while too much CO can impair oxygen carrying capacity of hemoglobin, leading to respiratory system disorders. To improve the controllability of CO delivery, recent attempts have been made to develop various CO-releasing molecules (CORMs), which can release CO with appropriate stimuli and maintain certain CO concentrations [23]. Nevertheless, COMRs are associated with the risk of spontaneous CO release during delivery and have demonstrated limited controllability, which would shorten the efficacy period and exacerbate toxicity.

The gas release controlled indirectly by photocatalytic reactions can increase the controllability of the therapeutic process. Zheng et al. have developed a photocatalytic platform for reducing CO2 to CO, using visible light, with targeted therapeutic efficacy [24]. However, the finite CO2 content in the microenvironment of wound infection will reduce the efficiency of photocatalytic CO production and hinder wound repair. Notably, CaCO3-based materials with pH responsiveness can increase the CO2 concentration in a weakly acidic environment at the site of wound infection, thereby realizing the targeted diagnosis and treatment [25]. CaCO3 can react with protons and dissolve to produce CO2 gas in an acidic environment (pH 6.8−7.2) [26]. Hence, CaCO3 can be used as a CO2 donor and incorporated into the photocatalytic CO2 reduction platform for improving the targeting and controllability during the treatment process.

In this study, we developed a pH/red light dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel for targeted wound infection treatment. We first prepared hybrid nanocomposite particles (CaCO3@AgCCN) consisting of CaCO3 and Z-scheme photocatalyst (Ag3PO4-decorated carbon dot g-C3N4, AgCCN), which were then loaded into a chitosan (CS) matrix to establish a pH/red light dual-responsive hydrogel (CaCO3@AgCCN/CS, CA/CS), as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The weakly acidic conditions due to bacterial growth would trigger the on-demand release of CO2 from the loaded CaCO3 in the hydrogel. The released CO2 would be subsequently catalytically converted to CO by AgCCN under 630-nm red light illumination to facilitate wound healing. The sterilization efficiency in vitro against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and the wound healing efficacy of full-thickness skin incisions were investigated.

All reagents were purchased from Energy Chemical, Macklin, Beijing Chemical, or Sigma-Aldrich. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were obtained from Nanjing Bianzhen Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). L929 murine fibroblast cell was purchased from Shanghai ASTRI Cell Resource Center, Chinese Academy of Sciences. BALB/c mice (7−8 weeks, female) were obtained from the Laboratory Animal Center, Basic Medical College of Jilin University. The study involving animals was according to the Animal Welfare and Research Ethics Committee of Jilin University.

We first prepared carbon dots (CDs) according to a reported method [24]. Typically, citric acid (4 g) and urea (1 g) were dispersed uniformly in 20 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-sealed autoclave and reacted at 170 °C for 18 h. Then, the resulting product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da molecular weight cutoff) for purification of the carbon dot dispersion.

10 g of urea and 5 mL of carbon dot dispersion were added to an alumina crucible, and the mixture was heated at 550 °C for 3 h to obtain carbon dot doped g-C3N4 (CCN). Then, 500 mg CCN and 276 mg AgNO3 were uniformly dispersed in deionized water by sonication. 200 mL of Na2HPO4·12H2O aqueous solution (2 mg/mL) was added dropwise to the above mixture while stirring. After continuous stirring for 5 h, the mixture was irradiated with an Xe lamp (500 W) for 1 h. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm, washing with deionized water three times, and vacuum drying at 40 °C for 24 h, the Ag3PO4-decorated CCN (AgCCN) was obtained.

44 mg CaCl2·2H2O and 50 mg AgCCN were dispersed in 45 mL ethanol under ultrasonication. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a vacuum oven containing 5 g NH4HCO3 powder and maintained for 24 h. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm, washed with deionized water three times, and dried in vacuum at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain CaCO3@AgCCN particles. Pure CaCO3 nanoparticles were obtained in a similar method without AgCCN [27,28].

0.36 g of CS was uniformly dispersed in 15 mL of acetic acid aqueous solution (1 %, v/v) under ultrasonication. Subsequently, NaOH solution was added dropwise to the mixture under agitation to adjust the pH to neutrality. Whereafter, CaCO3@AgCCN nanoparticles were incorporated into the above mixture, followed by vigorous stirring for 6 h to obtain a homogeneous mixture. The mixture was left at −20 °C for 24 h, and then immersed in NaOH solution at 4 °C for 2 h for CS gelation [29]. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried at −60 °C for 72 h to obtain dried nanocomposite hydrogels (CaCO3@AgCCN/CS). According to the mass fraction of CaCO3@AgCCN in these hydrogels, the prepared samples were labeled as CaCO3@AgCCN/CS-5 % (CA/CS-5 %), CaCO3@AgCCN/CS-10 % (CA/CS-10 %), CaCO3@AgCCN/CS-15 % (CA/CS-15 %) and CaCO3@AgCCN/CS-20 % (CA/CS-20 %), respectively. Moreover, CS hydrogels containing either CaCO3 or AgCCN were obtained using a similar method and named CaCO3/CS and AgCCN/CS, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CO2 and CO release
ce:section.ce:para: To visually observe the pH-responsive CO2 release behavior of CaCO3@AgCCN, CaCO3@AgCCN hybrid nanoparticles were deposited on the glass slides in PBS buffers at different pH (7.4, 6.8, and 6.0). Then the generation of bubbles was observed by an optical microscope. The pH-dependent CO2 generation from CA/CS hydrogel was monitored by a commercial CO2 detector (AZ77535, Taiwan ETS Product Service Co., Ltd). Briefly, CA/CS-20 % was added to a three-necked flask, and the detector was connected to the flask by the needle of a syringe after sealing. Subsequently, buffer solutions with various pH (PBS of pH 7.4 and 6.0) were injected into the flask through a syringe, and the CO2 concentrations produced were recorded at predetermined intervals.

CO release from CA/CS was measured using reduced hemoglobin (Hb) as a chemical probe [30]. Briefly, 3 mg Hb and 36 mg sodium dithionite were added into 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 6.0), and the mixture was bubbled with N2 for 15 min. Subsequently, CA/CS was added into the mixture and irradiated with 630-nm red light (30 W) for 10 min. The absorption spectra of this mixture were collected at intervals, and the released CO concentration (CCO) was estimated using the following Eq.:
C c o =
C
H b
×
528.6 ×
I
410 n m
− 304 ×
I
430 n m
216.5 ×
I
410 n m
+ 442.4 ×
I
430 n m
where C CO and C Hb are the released CO concentration and the initial Hb concentration, respectively, and I 410nm and I 430nm represent the absorbance at the wavelength of 410 and 430 nm, respectively.

To examine the swelling performance of these hydrogels (CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, CA/CS-20 %), dried hydrogels were cut in circular shape (7 mm in diameter) and weighed, denoted as m 0. Whereafter, these samples were immersed in 3 mL PBS for 5 min and weighed, denoted as m. The swelling ratio of hydrogel was calculated by comparing the weight before and after immersion: swelling ratio = m/m 0 × 100 %.

Stress-strain performance of these hydrogels was measured with a tensile tester (LR30K PLUS, LLOYD Instruments, Inc., UK). Dried hydrogels were cut into cylinders (diameter: 20 mm; height: 10 mm) and saturated in PBS. Subsequently, these hydrogels were compressed to the maximal compression strain of up to 80 % and the stress-strain curves were recorded.

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and E. coli (E. coli) were used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of these hydrogels [31]. After co-incubation with bacterial suspension (1 mL, 1 × 107 colony-forming units (CFU)·mL−1) for 2 h, the hydrogels (CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, and CA/CS-20 %) were illuminated with red light (630 nm, 10 min). One CA/CS-20 % sample was used as a control without light illumination. The agar plate colony counting method was adopted according to GB/T 31402-2015 standard to quantitatively assess the antibacterial efficiency of hydrogels [32]. Furthermore, the morphology of bacteria adhered to the hydrogel surface was observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM).

The CO production in bacterial culture medium during sterilization was detected using a CO probe (COP-1, Xi'an Qiyue Biological Technology Co., Ltd). After CA/CS-20 % hydrogel was co-cultured with 5 mL S. aureus suspension for 4 h, a mixture of COP-1 (1 μM) and PdCl2 (1 μM) was added. Then, the resulting mixture was illuminated under a red light (630 nm, 30 W), and the fluorescent emission spectra were collected at predetermined intervals. The mixture treated with a similar method in PBS solution (pH 7.4) was used as the control.

The cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8, Kumamoto, Japan) and live/dead staining assay were employed to assess the cytotoxicity of hydrogels [33]. Mouse fibroblast L929 cells were seeded in 96-well plates for 24 h (104 cells per well, 100 μL medium). Subsequently, hydrogels of CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, and CA/CS-20 % were gently transferred into the plates and irradiated with red light (630 nm) for 10 min. The growth culture medium without samples was used as a control. After co-incubation for 24 h, the hydrogels were removed, and CCK-8 reagent (10 μL) or calcein-AM/PI dual staining solution (50 μL) was added to each well. The absorbance of these specimens at 450 nm was recorded by a microplate reader, and live/dead cell staining images were collected via a BioTek CYTATION 5 image reader.

For the blood compatibility evaluation, the hydrogels were co-incubated with red blood cell (RBC) saline solution at 37 °C for 3 h. Deionized water- and saline-treated RBC represented the positive and negative controls, respectively. After centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, the absorbance of the released hemoglobin was measured at 540 nm using a microplate spectrophotometer. The hemolysis ratio was calculated using the following equation:
Hemolysis
ratio
( % )
=
A S
−
A N
A P
−
A N
× 100 %
where A S, A P, and A N are the absorbance for the sample, positive control, and negative control, respectively.

48 BALB/c mice (17.8 ± 0.5 g) were injected intraperitoneally with pentobarbital and randomly divided into eight groups (Control, CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, CA/CS-20 %). After shaving, full-thickness skin wounds with a diameter of 8 mm were constructed on the dorsal side of the rats. Then, 20 μL of S. aureus suspension (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was injected into defect areas to induce wound infection. The thus-created wounds were dressed with hydrogels and then irradiated with 630 nm light for 10 min. As the control group, one CA/CS-20 % covered wound was treated without illumination. These treated wounds were digitally photographed on days 1, 3, 5, and 7 to monitor the wound healing progress. On the 7th day, the infected tissues were retrieved, fixed in 10 % formalin buffer, stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E), and observed under a fluorescence microscope (DYF-680, Shanghai Point Optical Instrument Co., Ltd). Additionally, the detached infected tissues on the 7th day were cultured on LB agar plates to evaluate the anti-infection ability of these hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nanocomposite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: AgCCN, the g-C3N4 doped by carbon dot and Ag3PO4, was prepared according to the reported method [24], and it was chosen as a photocatalyst for CO2 reduction due to its exceptional photo-responsiveness, biocompatibility, and stability. As shown in Fig. 2a , the as-synthesized AgCCN appeared to have the typical nanosheet structure, with Ag3PO4 nanoparticles of about 5 nm evenly deposited on the surface. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of AgCCN showed the spacing between the adjacent lattice fringes was 0.3 nm corresponding to the (200) planes of Ag3PO4 crystal (Fig. S1). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) pattern of AgCCN exhibited the superimposed diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 (Fig. 2g), in which the characterization peak at 27.40° was indexed to the (002) diffraction plane of g-C3N4, and the peaks at 29.70°, 33.29°, and 36.59° were ascribed to the Ag3PO4 in cubic phase (JCPDS No. 06-0505) [34]. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CCN and AgCCN are shown in Fig. 2h, and the strong characteristic peaks in the range from 1200 cm−1 to 1700 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CN heterocycles [35,36]. Furthermore, the peak at 930 cm−1 indicated the presence of PO4 3− in AgCCN [37,38].

The successful combination of AgCCN and CaCO3 in CaCO3@AgCCN was confirmed by TEM. The nanosheet morphology of AgCCN was preserved in the hybrid particle (Fig. 2b), and CaCO3 particles of 40 nm in size appeared to be uniformly loaded in the nanosheets. Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images revealed the distribution of relevant elements (C, N, O, Ag, and Ca) in the CaCO3@AgCCN hybrid particle (Fig. 2c–f); in particular, the element Ca appeared to be only present where CaCO3 was located (Fig. 2f). The EDX spectrum of CaCO3@AgCCN exhibited the emblematic signals corresponding to C, N, O, Ag, and Ca (Fig. 2i), further pointing to the successful preparation of the hybrid nanoparticles.

We employed a simple freeze-drying method to prepare CA/CS nanocomposite hydrogels by incorporating CaCO3@AgCCN hybrid particles into a chitosan matrix [39]. Taking the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel as an example, a typical honeycomb-like structure with connected pores (on average 200 μm in size) was revealed in the SEM image (Fig. 3a ). The connected open pore structure could potentially promote the diffusion of wound tissue fluid into the swollen CA/CS hydrogel [40,41], hence facilitating the generation of CO. Notably, numerous nanoparticles were clearly discernible on the surface of the pore wall (Fig. 3a inset). The EDX elemental mapping further indicated the relatively uniform distribution of CaCO3@AgCCN hybrid particles in the nanocomposite hydrogel (Fig. 3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CO2 and CO release
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of CO2 bubbles from hybrid particles under different pH was visually observed with an optical microscope. As shown in Fig. S2a, no bubbles were observed when CaCO3@AgCCN was dispersed in a buffer solution of pH 7.4, and there was no obvious dissolution of the solid particle over a 5-min period, indicating no CO2 was produced under this condition. In contrast, pH 6.8 and 6.0 buffer solutions led to the formation of small bubbles around the hybrid particles, and the size and number of bubbles increased significantly over time. It was also evident that more bubbles were generated at pH 6.0 than at pH 6.8, obviously due to more CaCO3 decomposition at the lower pH.

We quantified the CO2 amount generated from the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel at different pH (7.4 and 6.0) by using a CO2 meter. At an acidic pH of 6.0, CO2 was rapidly produced in the presence of the hydrogel (Fig. S2b), and the CO2 concentration plateaued at ∼470 ppm in 8 min. At pH 7.4, a negligible amount of CO2 was generated from the hydrogel (Fig. S2b); but when the pH was adjusted to 6.0 at the 5-min mark, CO2 was again rapidly formed (Fig. S2c). Additionally, the CO2 release from CaCO3@AgCCN particles was detected to evaluated the CO2 release efficiency compared with CaCO3@AgCCN/CS hydrogel. As shown in Fig. S3a, CO2 was rapidly produced in the presence of the CaCO3@AgCCN particles at pH 6.0, and the CO2 levels accumulated to ∼4800 ppm in 10 min. Compared with CaCO3@AgCCN particles, the release rate of CO2 from CA/CS hydrogel was exhibited in Fig. S3b, the CO2 release rate gradually decreased at pH 6.0, and the release rate plateaued at ∼10.0 % in 8 min. The results clearly demonstrated that the formation of CO2 from the CA/CS hydrogels depended on pH, and the acidic pH can be used as an on-demand trigger to produce CO2 from the nanocomposite hydrogel.

The photocatalytic generation of CO in the presence of the nanocomposite hydrogel was investigated by a typical hemoglobin method by monitoring the UV–vis absorption of reduced hemoglobin (RHb) at 430 nm and carboxyhemoglobin (RHbCO) at 410 nm [42,43]. Due to the CO generated and released from the CA/CS hydrogels, RHb would be converted to RHbCO. Indeed, a significant shift from the 430-nm peak (RHb) to the 410-nm peak (RHbCO) was observed when the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel was illuminated under 630-nm light in 10 min (Fig. 3c). Without the red-light illumination, the peak shift was absent (Fig. 3d), unambiguously confirming that the 630-nm light was the trigger to generate CO from the nanocomposite hydrogel. The CO release behavior for various hydrogels is shown in Fig. 3e, and evidently, the presence of both CaCO3 and AgCCN in the hydrogel was crucial to the CO generation (those hydrogels of pure CS, CaCO3/CS, and AgCCN/CS led to negligible CO production even under 630-nm light illumination). In addition, the CO production could be tuned by the amount of the CaCO3@AgCCN hybrid particle, and the highest released CO amount reached 4.7 μM for the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel at the end of 10-min illumination. The above results have demonstrated that the dual responsiveness to the acidic pH and red light is the key for on-demand generation of CO in the CA/CS nanocomposite hydrogels. Furtherly, the on-demand CO generation was regulated by switching on/off the 630-nm light, as exhibited in Fig. S4. Notably, it is negligible CO generation in the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel without 630-nm light illumination, but when the illumination is turned on again, the production of CO increases immediately. It is proved that the CO output from CA/CS hydrogels can be well controlled by switching on/off the 630-nm light illumination time. Further, the yield of the released CO from the generated CO2 was evaluated. As exhibited in Fig. S5, the conversion efficiency of the released CO gradually increases, which plateaued at ∼0.043 % in 8 min with the 630-nm light illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water swelling and stress-strain property of nanocomposite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The water-absorbing property of hydrogels plays a significant role in maintaining the humidification of wound repair environment, promoting epithelialization, and accelerating wound healing. The swelling property of these hydrogels was evaluated by recording the volume and weight changes of dried hydrogel before and after water absorption. As shown in Fig. S6a, all hydrogels displayed a considerable volume change; the incorporation of hybrid particles only led to slight reduction of the water-absorbing capability, as the swelling ratio for all the hydrogels exceeded 2700 % (Fig. S6b), showing excellent water swelling properties.

In this work, the mechanical property of these hydrogels was estimated by a compression test. The compression stress-strain curves showed that the compressive strength of these hydrogels increases with the strain elevation (Fig. S6c). Notably, the nanoparticle-doped hydrogels possessed a higher strain compared with the pure CS, which might rely on the mechanical force enhancement effect of the nanocomposites [44]. Additionally, the doped hydrogels showed a puny difference in compressive strengths, which were ∼25 kPa when the maximum compressive strain reached 80%.

Light-treated sterilization test was performed by immersing these hydrogels into S. aureus and E. coli culture solutions and irradiating with 630-nm light for 10 min to assess the antimicrobial activities. As shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. S7, the bacterial growth in the presence of CS, CaCO3/CS, and AgCCN/CS hydrogels was as much as the control, regardless of whether there was light. In contrast, significantly reduced bacterial growth was observed with the CA/CS hydrogels; the higher the CaCO3@AgCCN amount in the hydrogel, the more bactericidal the hydrogel became. With 20 % of CaCO3@AgCCN, the CA/CS 20 % hydrogels showed a high antibacterial efficacy of 98 % (Fig. S8). Additionally, the antibacterial ability of the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel with different light duration was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5, the antibacterial ability of the CA/CS-20 % hydrogel was gradually enhanced with the extension of irradiation time. When the light exposure duration reached 8 min, the bacterial survival rate was <10 %. The above results clearly demonstrated that the antibacterial activity of the nanocomposite hydrogels could be directly correlated with the amount of CO generated under 630-nm light illumination.

To further verify the antimicrobial behavior of CA/CS hydrogels, S. aureus and E. coli were incubated with hydrogels, and the morphological variation of the bacterial cells was monitored by SEM after diverse treatments. As presented in Fig. 4b, the control group (CA/CS-20 % without illumination) did not bring about the apparent bacterial morphological variation. In contrast, CA/CS-treated bacteria with light illumination exhibited deformed bacterial morphology, and the cell membrane began to sunken and ablate. Notably, the bacteria cell deformation was aggravated with the increase of the doping amount of CaCO3@AgCCN. Obviously, the bacteria almost lost the original morphology when the CaCO3@AgCCN doping amount reached 20 %, and the cell membrane was ruptured, leading to the loss of intracellular contents and almost complete inactivation of the bacteria. Overall, the transformation of bacterial morphology was consistent with the results of the plate colony experiments, which proved that the antimicrobial ability of this CA/CS hydrogel was mainly attributed to the light-triggered CO release [17,45].

To further examine the effects of bacteria-growing cultural medium and red light illumination on the CO release behavior, a CO probe (COP-1) was used to detect the CO output during bacterial inactivation. As shown in Fig. 5a , the fluorescence intensity of the COP-1 probe increased gradually as the function of the time of red-light illumination, obviously indicating CO was continuously generated in the bacterial suspension (strong green fluorescence was observed after 10-min illumination, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of COP-1 in PBS at pH 7.4 hardly changed (Fig. 5b), confirming that an acidic pH (such as bacteria-growing medium) is the on-demand trigger for the CO generation. In brief, the antibacterial mechanism of CA/CS hydrogel can be summarized in two steps: (1) the weakly acidic environment where bacteria grew led to the generation of CO2 from CaCO3@AgCCN; (2) CO2 was subsequently converted into CO, photo-catalytically via red light illumination, for bactericidal action (Fig. 5c). The concentration of the released CO can be readily controlled via adjusting the time of the red light illumination, allowing CO to be safely used for therapeutic treatment in would dressings and other medical fields.

Biocompatibility is a precondition for the nanocomposite hydrogels to be used in the biomedical field. The cytotoxicity of CA/CS hydrogel was evaluated in L929 fibroblasts by live/dead staining and a standard CCK-8 assay [46]. As shown in Fig. 6a , vivid cell activity was observed for all the tested samples. The CCK-8 assay further revealed that the viability of L929 fibroblasts on various hydrogels was similar to that of the control group (Fig. 6b). Additionally, compared with the negative control, all hydrogel samples exhibited no apparent hemolysis when in contact with RBCs (Fig. 6c), indicating good blood compatibility of these hydrogels. The hemolysis percentage of all the nanocomposite hydrogels was below 2 %, which meets the requirements of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 10993-4 [47].

The CA/CS nanocomposite hydrogels have demonstrated on-demand bactericidal property; coupled with their biocompatibility, these hydrogels may hold great promise to be used as a dressing for infectious wounds. S. aureus infected mouse full-thickness defect model was built to estimate the capability of these hydrogels for wound healing. The wound treated by CA/CS-20 % without light illumination was performed as a control group, and CS, CaCO3/CS, AgCCN/CS, CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, and CA/CS-20 % with 630 nm illumination were regarded as the experimental groups. Fig. 7a shows the representative photographs of mouse wounds in different groups after 1–7-day treatment, and the wound size became smaller over time. On day 1, the control, CS, CaCO3/CS, and AgCCN/CS treatment groups all showed significant wound suppuration and redness, and the wound area was still higher than 20 % on day 7 (Fig. 7c). In contrast, the CA/CS treatment groups led to more rapid wound healing; it also became obvious that the higher the CaCO3@AgCCN content in the hydrogel, the faster the wound healing. In particular, there was no suppuration and redness for the CA/CS-20 % treatment group even on the first day, and by the 7th day, the size of the wound has shrunken to about 5 % of the original size and there was no scab or abscess. Furthermore, the bacterial number separated from the infected wounds was consistent with the result of visible wound infection photographs (Fig. 7b). On day seven, the relative bacterial survival rate after the treatment with CA/CS-5 %, CA/CS-10 %, CA/CS-15 %, and CA/CS-20 % was 49.7 %, 23.6 %, 9.4 %, and 1.4 %, respectively (Fig. 7d). Meanwhile, the weight gain for all the mouses in all treatment groups appeared to be similar over the 7-day period (Fig. 7e), revealing the minimal side effects for the nanocomposite hydrogels in the treatment process.

It is well known that wound inflammation is closely related to tissue injury, repair, and regeneration [48]. The H&E staining assay of these wound tissues on day 7 was implemented to evaluate the re-epithelialization, re-vascularization, synthesis of connective tissue, and infiltration of inflammatory cells. For the groups of control, CS, CaCO3/CS, and AgCCN/CS, disorganized granulation tissues, disconnected epithelial layer, and numerous inflammatory cells were observed (Fig. 8 ). For the CA/CS-5 % and CA/CS-10 % groups, but there were still some inflammatory cells as well as anomalous connective tissues. In contrast, the treatment groups of CA/CS-15 % and CA/CS-20 % exhibited a significantly decreased number of inflammatory cells, and the epidermis and dermis were tightly connected, with new blood vessels and hair follicles formation (Fig. 8). In addition, the epithelial layer and granulation tissue became organized, which showed no obvious difference from the structure of healthy tissue. The above results have clearly shown that the CA/CS nanocomposite hydrogels significantly accelerated wound healing through antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory actions under 630-nm light illumination. The excellent wound healing effect could be attributed to the antibacterial and anti-inflammatory capabilities of CO through on-demand release from nanocomposite hydrogels [49].

In summary, a nanocomposite hydrogel containing CaCO3@AgCCN was developed successfully. The resultant CA/CS hydrogel would generate sufficient CO2 in the bacteria-growing, mildly acidic environment, which could be catalytically converted to CO triggered by 630-nm light illumination for antimicrobial treatment. The production of CO can be well adjusted by controlling the illumination duration (0–10 min) and CaCO3@AgCCN content (mass fraction 0–20 %), toward maximizing therapeutic effect while maintaining the safe dosage of CO. Based on the on-demand release of CO, CA/CS hydrogel exhibited strong bactericidal and anti-inflammatory effects in vitro and in vivo studies, which was essential for facilitating wound healing. The biocompatibility of this dual-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel showed a satisfactory effect even with light illumination. This CA/CS hydrogel with the ability to safely control CO generation can achieve effective therapeutic outcomes while ensuring biosafety for potential use in biomedical applications such as antibacterial and anti-inflammatory dressings.

Jing Sun: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Resources. Fengjun Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Qiqi Lu: Investigation, Data curation. Wei Ye: Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Sen Liu: Validation, Data curation. Jingjing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Chao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Jie Zhao: Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Weihua Ming: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52073111 and No. 52103162), the National Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. 22KJB430001).
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105133.
